title: Race-Held-Out Expert Blend With Beta or Local Calibration
summary: F1 pit probabilities are regime-heterogeneous: early-stint dry laps, late one-stop races, wet crossover laps, and traffic-driven undercuts do not share the same score-to-probability map.
rationale: F1 pit probabilities are regime-heterogeneous: early-stint dry laps, late one-stop races, wet crossover laps, and traffic-driven undercuts do not share the same score-to-probability map. Calibration literature suggests global Platt-style fixes can be too rigid, while isotonic can overfit; beta calibration or local calibration trees are better candidates for distorted GBDT scores.
implementation_hint: Keep model training mostly unchanged, but score every base model with `LeaveOneGroupOut` on `Race_Year` or a stricter grouped split, then fit a small calibrator per regime bucket such as dry/wet, early/mid/late race, and stint index. Compare global isotonic vs beta calibration vs a tiny calibration tree on OOF predictions only. Blend calibrated experts rather than raw scores.
expected_effect: Often improves leaderboard robustness more than raw CV, especially if current probabilities are mis-scaled across race regimes or shifted test composition.
risk: AUC is rank-based, so calibration alone may not move local CV much; over-partitioned calibrators can become noisy on rare wet or late-race slices.
